1: 'I have come to my garden, my sister, my bride' — the beloved accepts the invitation of 4:16; his arrival completes the movement of ch. 4 from sealed garden to open welcome. The gathering of myrrh, spice, honeycomb, honey, wine, and milk enacts the abundance promised in 4:13-15. The closing 'eat, friends; drink deeply, be drunk with love' — the wedding feast shared with companions; the banquet context (cf. 2:4) places the consummation within community celebration. The language of eating, drinking, and intoxication was standard in ANE descriptions of both feasting and sexual fulfillment.
2: 'I slept, but my heart was awake' — the second dream-vision begins; the heart (*lev*) watching while the body sleeps reflects the ANE understanding of the heart as the active, willing center of the person rather than the passive recipient of experience. The beloved's knock and voice (*qol*) is a literary device: we hear the words he would say in the dream, not the actual knock. The four address terms (*sister, darling, dove, perfect one*) accumulate into the fullest possible declaration of love. The 'head drenched with dew' locates him outside, in the night air — waiting.
3: 'I have taken off my robe — how can I put it back on?' — the *ketoneth* (robe, the basic garment worn next to the body) was taken off before sleeping. Re-dressing to open the door was not a trivial act; it also meant re-dirtying feet already washed for bed. The beloved's hesitation is humanly authentic — the gap between desire and the cost of action. In honor-shame terms, going to the door at night in a state of undress also carried social risk; the beloved weighs her ease against her desire and, in the dream, hesitates fatally.
4: 'My beloved thrust his hand through the latch opening' — the mechanical description of ancient door latches: a hand could be inserted through an opening to operate the latch from outside; the *chor* (hole, opening) in the door for the hand is a literal description. The beloved's act of attempting entry creates the *hamah* (intense longing, literally: 'my inward parts were troubled for him') in the woman — the same word used for the roar of surging waters (Ps 46:3; Isa 17:12) and the noise of a tumultuous crowd: an overwhelming physical sensation.
5: 'My fingers with flowing myrrh on the handles of the lock' — when the woman rises to open the door, her hands drip with *mor over* (flowing/liquid myrrh, the finest grade of myrrh as opposed to solid lumps). This may be the costly perfume that the beloved applied before the encounter, now transferred to the door — a fragrant trace of his presence. Myrrh was applied to the body and hair as luxury perfume; its presence on the door handles is both aromatic and evocative of the beloved's nearness.
6: 'I opened to my beloved, but he had gone' — the moment of missed connection; love's most painful experience. The silence that follows ('I called but he did not answer') is the divine hiddenness translated into the register of human love. Similar silences appear in Psalm 22:1-2 (my God, why have you forsaken me — I cry but you do not answer) and Isaiah 55:6 (seek the LORD while he may be found). The Song's dream-world reflects the actual experience of longing and absence that characterizes both human love and Israel's experience of the hidden God.
7: 'The guards found me, beat me, wounded me; the watchmen on the walls stripped off my shawl' — the contrast with the neutral encounter in 3:3 is stark. The same *shomerim* (watchmen/guards) who merely encountered her before now beat (*hikkuni*) and wound (*peshu*) her; the *shomerim homaot* (watchmen on the walls) are a different group. In the social world of the text, a woman found wandering alone at night in a disheveled state could be treated as a vagrant or a woman of ill repute; her *redid* (veil or shawl) is stripped — an act of social shaming, exposing what should be covered. The beloved risks public humiliation for love.
8: 'Tell him I am faint with love' — the woman's message to be conveyed via the daughters of Jerusalem: *holat ahavah ani* (I am sick with love). The lovesickness (*chalah me'ahavah*) is a medical state in ANE literature: Egyptian physicians described the physical symptoms of love's absence (racing pulse, listlessness, fever) as a genuine malady. The woman's willingness to broadcast her lovestruck state to strangers reflects the intensity that overrides the normal restraint of the honor-shame system.
9: 'What makes your beloved better than any other lover?' — the daughters of Jerusalem's challenge prompts the extended *wasf* of vv. 10-16. Their question is the standard competitive honor discourse of the ancient world: each person's beloved is supposed to be the best, but what is the specific *excellence* (*mah*) that distinguishes him? The title 'O most beautiful of women' (*hayafa bannashim*) repeats 1:8, confirming her pre-eminence as the context for the challenge.
10: 'My beloved is radiant and rugged, outstanding among ten thousand men' — *tzach ve'adom* (radiant/white and ruddy/red): the combination of fair skin and a healthy reddish complexion. David was described with similar terms (*admoni*, ruddy, with beautiful eyes, 1 Sam 16:12); Lamentations 4:7 describes the Nazirites as 'rudder than rubies, more radiant than sapphires.' *Dagul merevavah* (outstanding among ten thousand, literally: distinguished/bannered among myriads) — the beloved stands out from an army of men as a standard-bearer stands from the crowd.
11: 'His head is like purest gold; his hair wavy and dark as a raven' — gold (*paz*, the finest refined gold) for the head; *taltalim* (wavy, looping curls) for the hair, dark as *orev* (raven) — the raven's black was the standard of intense darkness in Hebrew (cf. Lam 4:8, where blackened skin signals deprivation; Prov 7:9, the dark of the night). The beloved's combination of golden (royally precious) head and raven-dark hair is a description of maximum contrast and richness.
12: 'His eyes are like doves beside streams of water — bathed in milk, perfectly set' — the dove-eye comparison from 1:15 is now applied by her to him; the mutuality of the imagery is characteristic. 'Bathed in milk' (*rachatzot bechalav*) = brilliantly white or perhaps watery/clear; 'perfectly set' (*yoshvot al milat*) may refer to the eye sitting perfectly in its socket, or to gems set in their bezels. The elaborate precision of the description reflects the ANE *wasf* tradition's cataloging of beauty.
13: 'His cheeks are like beds of spice, banks of sweet herbs; his lips are lilies dripping with flowing myrrh' — the cheeks (*lechaiv*) described as aromatic spice beds: fragrant and carefully cultivated. The lips (*sefatav*) dripping with flowing myrrh: the beloved's speech is as fragrant as the finest perfume; 'lilies' for the lips suggests softness and color. The aromatic body description follows the ANE convention of comparing the beloved's various features to luxury goods — spices, gold, ivory, precious stones.
14: 'His hands are gold cylinders set with jewels; his torso carved ivory overlaid with sapphires' — the gold *gliley zahav* (gold cylinders or rollings) set with *tarshish* (a gem, possibly beryl or chrysolite from Tarshish in Spain) describe massive, ringed forearms. The torso (*me'av*) described as carved ivory (*shen*) — elephant ivory was the luxury material par excellence in the ancient near east — overlaid (*muleefet*) with sapphires (*sappirim*; possibly lapis lazuli rather than modern sapphire). The description turns the human body into a work of art in precious materials.
15: 'His legs are like columns of alabaster, set in sockets of gold; his form like Lebanon, magnificent as the cedars' — alabaster (*shesh*) columns set in gold sockets describe the architectural grandeur of Solomon's palace and temple (1 Kgs 7:15-22); the beloved's legs are as strong and magnificent as those temple columns. 'Magnificent as the cedars' (*bechur ka'arazim*) — the cedar of Lebanon was the superlative of natural beauty and majesty; the beloved's overall form has the scale and grandeur of a cedar forest.
16: 'His mouth is sweetness itself; he is altogether desirable. This is my beloved and this is my friend' — *cheeko mamtaqim* (his mouth/palate is sweetnesses, plural of intensity) — the plural expresses excess of quality. The closing declaration 'this is my beloved and this is my friend' (*zeh dodi vezeh re'i*) is the climax of the *wasf*: after all the royal and artistic comparisons, the final word is not 'king' or 'warrior' but *dodi* (my beloved) and *re'i* (my friend). The integration of erotic love (*dod*) and friendship (*re'a*) is the Song's fullest statement of what ideal human love contains.
